analysis.summary: Aetna denied Jane Doe's lumbar spine MRI (claim CLM-2025-99999, member AET-555123456) citing lack of medical necessity per clinical guidelines.
analysis.strategy: Challenge the denial by obtaining a detailed Letter of Medical Necessity from the treating provider documenting failed conservative treatments, functional limitations, and specific clinical indicators that meet or exceed Aetna's own clinical policy bulletins for lumbar MRI. Request a copy of the specific clinical guideline Aetna applied and challenge any deviation from the ordering physician's clinical judgment. If internal appeal fails, escalate to an Independent Medical Review given that lumbar MRI denials based on medical necessity are frequently overturned when adequate clinical documentation is presented.
keyArgs: Aetna's denial fails to account for the treating physician's direct clinical evaluation and professional judgment, which carries significant weight under established medical necessity standards

Lumbar spine MRI is consistent with Aetna's own Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) criteria when conservative treatment of 4-6 weeks has been attempted and failed, or when red flag symptoms are present — the denial letter does not specify which criteria were unmet

A blanket 'not medically necessary' denial without identifying the specific guideline provision or clinical finding that was deficient violates the requirement for a clear and specific denial rationale under ERISA Section 503

Denial of diagnostic imaging that prevents accurate diagnosis may constitute a denial of covered benefits, as treatment cannot be appropriately tailored without imaging to rule out serious pathology such as disc herniation, stenosis, or malignancy

Under ERISA's full and fair review requirement, Aetna must provide all clinical criteria used in the denial decision and allow the claimant to respond to any new evidence or rationale introduced at the appeal stage
nextSteps: Immediately contact Aetna at the member services number on your insurance card to request the complete denial letter, the specific clinical guideline or policy bulletin applied, and the credentials of the reviewing clinician

Obtain a detailed Letter of Medical Necessity from the ordering provider documenting diagnosis codes, symptom duration, functional impairment, all prior conservative treatments attempted and their outcomes, and specific clinical reasons why MRI is required

Compile supporting clinical records including office visit notes, physical therapy records, prior imaging, medication history, and any documented red flag symptoms (e.g., neurological deficits, bowel/bladder dysfunction, unexplained weight loss)

File a formal Level 1 internal appeal in writing to Aetna referencing claim CLM-2025-99999 and member ID AET-555123456, attaching all clinical documentation and a point-by-point rebuttal of the medical necessity denial

If the internal appeal is denied or not resolved within 30 days, file for an Independent Medical Review (IMR) or External Review through your state insurance commissioner or under ERISA, depending on plan type
Document all communications with Aetna including dates, representative names, and call reference numbers to preserve a record for potential escalation or legal action
escalationPath: Level 1 Internal Appeal to Aetna's Appeals and Grievance Unit with full clinical documentation package
External Independent Medical Review through the applicable state Department of Insurance or federal external review process under the ACA/ERISA
File a complaint with the state Department of Insurance and/or consult an ERISA or insurance bad faith attorney if external review is unfavorable or if Aetna fails to follow procedural requirements
legalCitations: ERISA Section 503 (29 U.S.C. § 1133) — requires plan to provide specific reasons for denial and a full and fair review process
29 C.F.R. § 2560.503-1 — DOL claims procedure regulations requiring disclosure of all clinical criteria used and opportunity to respond
ACA Section 2719 (42 U.S.C. § 300gg-19) — mandates internal appeals and external review rights for health plan denials
Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. v. Bruch, 489 U.S. 101 (1989) — establishes de novo review standard for ERISA benefit denials where plan does not grant discretionary authority
evidenceNeeded: Detailed Letter of Medical Necessity from the ordering physician specifying diagnosis, symptom duration, failed conservative treatments, and clinical rationale for MRI
Complete clinical notes documenting the patient's history of lumbar complaints, physical examination findings, and any neurological symptoms
Records of prior conservative treatment attempts (physical therapy, medications, chiropractic care) with dates and outcomes demonstrating failure to resolve symptoms
Copy of Aetna's applicable Clinical Policy Bulletin for lumbar spine MRI to identify and directly rebut the specific criteria cited as unmet
Any relevant prior authorizations, referral records, or specialist notes that corroborate the medical necessity of advanced imaging

letter:
# FORMAL APPEAL OF ADVERSE BENEFIT DETERMINATION

---

**Jane Doe**
test@coverfight.ai

May 21, 2026

**Aetna**
Appeals and Grievances Department

---

**RE: Formal First-Level Appeal — Adverse Benefit Determination**
Member Name: Jane Doe
Member ID: AET-555123456
Claim Number: CLM-2025-99999
Procedure: MRI of Lumbar Spine
Denial Reason: Not Medically Necessary

---

## I. FORMAL STATEMENT OF APPEAL

To Whom It May Concern:

This letter constitutes a formal first-level appeal of Aetna's adverse benefit determination denying coverage for an MRI of the lumbar spine for member Jane Doe (Member ID: AET-555123456; Claim No. CLM-2025-99999) on the stated ground that the procedure was "not medically necessary per clinical guidelines."

This denial is factually unsupported, procedurally deficient, and legally inadequate under applicable federal law. Jane Doe, through this appeal, demands immediate reconsideration, reversal of the denial, and approval of benefits for the lumbar spine MRI. If Aetna fails to reverse this determination, Ms. Doe expressly reserves all rights to pursue independent external review under the Affordable Care Act and all other remedies available under federal and state law.

---

## II. MEDICAL NECESSITY ARGUMENT

### A. The Denial Fails to Identify Any Specific Clinical Criterion That Was Not Met

Aetna's denial states only that the lumbar spine MRI was "not medically necessary per clinical guidelines." This conclusory statement is wholly insufficient. Aetna's own Clinical Policy Bulletin criteria for lumbar spine MRI recognize medical necessity where, among other circumstances, a patient has undergone a course of conservative treatment of approximately four to six weeks that has failed to produce adequate relief, or where red flag symptoms are present suggesting serious underlying pathology.

The denial letter does not identify which specific criterion was unmet, does not reference the applicable Clinical Policy Bulletin by number or version, and does not cite the specific clinical finding — or absence thereof — that led to the determination that those criteria were not satisfied. Without this specificity, it is impossible for the member or her treating physician to meaningfully respond to the denial, which is precisely the type of procedural deficiency prohibited under federal law.

### B. The Treating Physician's Clinical Judgment Has Not Been Adequately Considered

The denial does not indicate that Aetna's reviewing clinician gave appropriate weight to the direct clinical evaluation and professional judgment of the treating physician. The treating physician, who has personally examined Jane Doe, evaluated her history, assessed her symptoms, and ordered the lumbar spine MRI based on that direct clinical knowledge, is in a materially superior position to render a judgment about medical necessity compared to a reviewer conducting a paper-only or records-based review. Aetna's clinical reviewer has not examined Ms. Doe. Wholesale substitution of that reviewer's judgment for that of the treating physician — without identifying what specific clinical evidence was disregarded and why — does not satisfy any recognized standard of medically appropriate review.

### C. Denial of Diagnostic Imaging Constitutes a Denial of Covered Benefits

Lumbar spine MRI is a diagnostic procedure. Its purpose is to enable the treating physician to identify or rule out serious pathology — including disc herniation, lumbar stenosis, nerve root compression, and spinal malignancy — that cannot be reliably excluded through physical examination alone. Denying this imaging does not simply defer a treatment decision; it actively prevents accurate diagnosis and, by extension, appropriate treatment. Where a covered member presents with lumbar symptoms warranting physician-ordered diagnostic imaging, denial of that imaging constitutes a denial of covered benefits under the plan. The clinical and legal consequence of preventing accurate diagnosis — including the potential for delayed identification of serious or progressive conditions — is entirely Aetna's responsibility if this denial is permitted to stand.

---

## III. LEGAL BASIS FOR APPEAL

This appeal is grounded in the following federal legal authorities, each of which Aetna is obligated to comply with:

**1. ERISA Section 503 (29 U.S.C. § 1133)**
Section 503 of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act requires that every employee benefit plan provide adequate written notice to any participant or beneficiary whose claim is denied, setting forth the specific reasons for denial in a manner calculated to be understood by the claimant, and affording a reasonable opportunity for a full and fair review of the adverse determination. Aetna's denial — which states only a conclusory finding of "not medically necessary" without identifying the specific guideline provision, clinical criterion, or clinical evidence at issue — does not satisfy the specificity requirement of Section 503 and is therefore procedurally deficient on its face.

**2. 29 C.F.R. § 2560.503-1 — Department of Labor Claims Procedure Regulations**
The DOL's implementing regulations under ERISA require that an adverse benefit determination identify all clinical criteria relied upon in making the determination, disclose any internal rules, guidelines, or protocols applied, and provide the claimant a meaningful opportunity to respond. Aetna must disclose, upon request and as part of this appeal, the complete clinical criteria and any Clinical Policy Bulletin or coverage guideline applied to this claim. Ms. Doe expressly demands that disclosure now. Furthermore, to the extent Aetna introduces any new evidence, rationale, or clinical criteria at the appeal stage that were not present in the original denial, Ms. Doe must be provided the opportunity to review and respond to that new information before a final determination is issued.

**3. ACA Section 2719 (42 U.S.C. § 300gg-19) — Mandated Internal Appeals and External Review**
The Affordable Care Act mandates that health plans provide both an internal appeals process and access to an independent external review process for adverse benefit determinations. Jane Doe hereby places Aetna on notice that she intends to exercise her right to independent external review if this internal appeal is not resolved in her favor. Aetna is required to provide written notice of the external review process and the applicable deadlines as part of any adverse determination at this stage.

**4. Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. v. Bruch, 489 U.S. 101 (1989)**
The Supreme Court's decision in *Firestone* establishes that benefit denials under ERISA plans are subject to de novo review by a federal court where the plan does not unambiguously grant discretionary authority to the plan administrator. Under de novo review, a court independently evaluates whether the denial was correct — it does not defer to Aetna's interpretation. Ms. Doe reserves all rights to seek judicial review of this determination, and Aetna should be prepared to defend the clinical and procedural adequacy of this denial under that standard.

---

## IV. DEMAND FOR REVIEWER INFORMATION

Pursuant to 29 C.F.R. § 2560.503-1 and the procedural requirements of a full and fair review, Jane Doe formally demands that Aetna provide, in writing and within the timeframe specified below, the full name, professional degree, specialty, board certification status, and licensure information of every clinical reviewer, medical director, and utilization management professional who was involved in making the adverse benefit determination on Claim No. CLM-2025-99999. This includes, without limitation, the physician or clinician who conducted the medical necessity review and any supervising medical director who approved or affirmed the denial. This information is required to assess whether the reviewing clinician possesses the appropriate specialty expertise to evaluate a lumbar spine MRI and to evaluate any conflict of interest in the review process.

---

## V. REQUEST FOR PEER-TO-PEER REVIEW

Jane Doe, through her treating physician, formally requests an immediate peer-to-peer consultation between her treating physician and Aetna's medical director or the clinical reviewer responsible for this denial. This peer-to-peer review must occur before any final determination is issued on this appeal. The purpose of this consultation is to provide Aetna's reviewer with direct access to the clinical basis for the treating physician's medical necessity determination, including clinical findings, symptom history, and the diagnostic purpose of the lumbar spine MRI. Peer-to-peer review is an established and widely recognized mechanism for resolving medical necessity disputes, and Aetna's failure to offer or conduct such a review — particularly in light of a facially conclusory denial — would further evidence the inadequacy of its review process.

To schedule the peer-to-peer review, Aetna should contact Ms. Doe at test@coverfight.ai, and Ms. Doe will coordinate directly with her treating physician's office.

---

## VI. RIGHT TO INDEPENDENT EXTERNAL REVIEW

As stated above, Jane Doe expressly preserves and asserts her right to independent external review pursuant to ACA Section 2719 (42 U.S.C. § 300gg-19) and any applicable state external review laws. If this appeal is denied, or if Aetna fails to render a timely decision, Ms. Doe will immediately file for external review with the appropriate independent review organization. Aetna is required by federal law to cooperate fully with that process, to submit all relevant documentation to the external reviewer within required timeframes, and to be bound by the external reviewer's determination. Any attempt by Aetna to delay, obstruct, or impede the external review process will be treated as a violation of federal law.

---

## VII. DEMAND FOR WRITTEN RESPONSE WITHIN 30 DAYS

Aetna is hereby directed to provide a complete written response to this appeal — including either a full reversal of the adverse determination and approval of benefits, or a final adverse benefit determination supported by specific clinical rationale, identified guideline provisions, and all information required under 29 C.F.R. § 2560.503-1 — **no later than 30 days from the date of this letter, being June 20, 2026.**

Any final adverse determination issued in response to this appeal must include:

- The specific clinical criteria and guideline provisions upon which the denial is based, identified by name, number, and version;
- The name and credentials of every reviewer involved in the appeal determination;
- A complete description of the external review process and applicable deadlines; and
- A copy of all documents, records, and other information relevant to the claim, to the extent not previously provided.

Failure to respond within this timeframe, or issuance of a final denial that does not comply with the above requirements, will be treated as a continued violation of ERISA Section 503 and DOL claims procedure regulations and will be included in any subsequent judicial or regulatory filing.

---

## VIII. ENCLOSURES

The following documents are enclosed with this appeal:

1. Copy of Aetna's adverse benefit determination letter for Claim No. CLM-2025-99999
2. Treating physician's letter of medical necessity for lum
